title: CGIL Seminar W25 – Dr. Dorret Boomsma and Nikki Hubers
text:
this afternoon. Uh good afternoon Seel.
This week we are pleased to welcome
Professor Dr. Dret Binsma and Mickey a
professor and a PhD student at Fria
University pres presenting for us. Uh
professor Boomma is a twin researcher
and a quantitative geneticist at the
Fria University Amsterdam. She is a
member of the Royal Dutch Academy of
Sciences, the Royal Holland Society of
Sciences and Humanities and the Academia
Europeia. In 2022, she became a doctor
on Kaza at faculty of medicine
University of Helsinki. In the 1980s,
she founded the Netherlands Quinn
Register together with Professor O or
Beaki, which formed the foundation for
her research into the ideology of
population variation and human complex
traits and led to numerous publications
and over 70 PhD dissertations under her
supervision. Her work has led to
national and international recognition,
including the Spinosa Prize, which is
the highest scientific award in the
Netherlands. She initiated and
participates in multiple twin and GB was
boss consoria and Nikki Hoovers is a PhD
student of professor Bones Matt Afria
and has a background in biomedical
sciences and behavioral genetics. Her
PhD focuses on the ideology of twinning
and she studies the relationship between
dzygotic twinning and fertility through
genetically based methods such as
genetic correlations and polygenic
scores. Another important aspect of her
research is translating genetic findings
into biological insights by studying and
integrating multiple omics data
including epigenomics, transcrytoics and
metabolomics. Today they will be
presenting what we currently know about
human twinning and its relation to
fertility. So you are welcome to start.
Are you ready?
You're looking sideways on my screen.
Are you looking at me? I am ready. Oh
okay. Okay. Thanks so much for this very
nice uh
introduction and uh I'm especially
excited to present uh to a group of
um people who study other speeies than
humans. So I will begin uh today with
giving you an update about what we know
about the genetics of twinning in
humans. So not talking about the twin
method as a design but trying to unravel
why some
women give birth to multiples and most
of us
don't. So it it's of course very
important to realize that there are two
types of twins uh dicycotic twins and
monocycotic twins. The dicycotic twins
arise after a double
ovulation and are
independently
fertilized and it's a trait that shows
huge global
differences. Uh dicycodic twinning is
very common on the West African coast
with as many as one in 30 or 40 birds
being a multiple bird. and it's very
rare in Asian countries. It's a complex
trait. It doesn't show a simple
mandelian inheritance, but it's clearly
segregating in families and in
pedigrees. Um, in multiple species,
candidate genes have been
identified, which however largely, not
completely, but largely failed to
replicate in
humans. And after some decades of doing
segregation
analysis, candidate genes and linkage
studies, we finally could turn to
genomewide association studies. And this
has given us the first insights into the
genes underlying twinning in
humans. The story is completely
different for monocycodic twinning.
There's no clear implication in
epidemiological studies that the trade
runs in
families except for some very rare
pedigrees like think of a few pedigrees
worldwide where the trade seems to be
segregating in
families. So this is basically the
outline of our talk. I will talk a
little bit about the history of research
into twinning in humans and um what we
know about its
heritability. then introduce the
genomewide association studies that we
carried out where we documented the
first uh significant evidence for genes
involved in human twinning and also talk
a little bit about our work in
monocycotic twinning and what we hope to
achieve in future projects and then I'm
very happy I can hand over to Nikki who
will talk about her work on polygenic
score prediction or polygenic risk
scores.
and also update you on the new project
on the new G was that she is running
currently and hopes to complete it over
summer.
So the Bible almost for our work is this
book that was published in 1970 by uh
Balmer, the biology of
twinning. And it beautifully documents
the global differences in twinning rates
that I just mentioned to
you and also lists a number of other
risk
factors. The risk of giving birth to
dicycotic twins is substantially higher
with increasing maternal age and there
is a very well doumented risk to female
relatives which correspond to a
heritability of about 28% for twinning
in humans.
Um and this estimate was obtained by us
by um converting the recurrent rates
that were published by Bulmer for some
quite old data sets to tetraoric
correlations and then doubling the
sister correlation to achieve a
heritability of 28%.
Then a few years ago, our Australian
colleague David Duffy found some
fantastic large data sets online and
estimated the heritability for twinning
in very large multi-generation
pedigrees. So and when I say
multigeneration the largest one uh spans
19 generations and in the next slide I
have u an example of such a pedigree.
This is um from an African uh pedigree
and David
estimated that the inheritance patterns
in these pedigrees was compatible with
the heritability of around 20%.
Since we could not distinguish in these
pedigrees between mono and dicycotic
twins although the prevalence of
dicycotic twins will be much larger than
that of monocycotic twins. Um it seems
quite consistent with the estimate that
we derived from the data in the Bulmer
book. And then we have a new project in
progress um in which we look at the
brother brother sister sister and
brother sister correlations for being
the parents of twins in register white
data and the first results have come in
from
Norway. If we focus on parents who are
the parents of confirmed dicycotic
twins, the correlations between brothers
are not different from zero. In opposite
sex siblings, they're not different from
zero. But in sister pairs, they're
about.15. So a herit consistent with a
heritability of 30%. And this is
populationwide in Norway.
So the first
step for a dicycotic pregnancy must be a
double ovulation in the mother and uh we
know there are such mothers. This is an
example of a large Dutch family that
moved uh during the 1950s from the
Netherlands uh to the
US. And this
mother had a total of 25 children. Also
illustrating the association between
higher fertility parity and twinning
which included five sets of
twins.
Our colleague uh Nick
Martin followed a cohort of women for a
period of 12
months. And the women selected into the
study were all mothers of multiple pairs
of dicycotic twins. And um each month uh
they came in for ultrasound.
And
this summarizes the months. Oh, I'm

see each month and you see for this
particular mother that nearly every
cycle she has uh a multiple ovulation
can be on both sides. It can be more on
one side than on the other side. But she
is clearly a super ovulator.
Um if we turn to um other species, we
know quite a bit about the inheritance
of uh twinning in
sheep. The heritability of litter size
in sheep seems to be quite consistent
with the heritability of uh dicycotic
twinning in humans.
But if we look at the heritability of
ovulation rate in sheep, then the
heritability is quite a bit higher. So
if we could consistently measure this in
humans, it would probably be easier to
get to the genes for twinning, then we
currently have to do with uh a phenotype

giving birth to twins. Of course, there
are other steps involved in having
dicycodic twins or having twins. It's
not only realizing the pregnancy, but
also being able or having the capacity
to bring a double pregnancy to term.
So if we switch focus for a moment and
look at what we know about environment
and twinning, um Darwin already observed
that the amount of food affects sheep
who on high mountains never produce
twins, but when they are moved to land,
they frequently have twins.
and a Finnish colleague who worked in
the Netherlands for a long time looked
at the role of nutrition in producing
twins um in what we could call natural

quite
harsh. He looked at periods of

Sweden, and the Netherlands. And I'm
going to focus here, um, on the
Netherlands where in 1944, 1945,
um, the western part of the country
experienced the Dutch hunger winter. Uh
this was after a large train strike in
the
Netherlands after which the Germans
decided if you strike we will not
transport food to the Netherlands any
longer. But what Alder Ericson could do,
he could compare the western part of the
country where there was very severe
deprivation and then you should think of
about having 700 calories of food intake
per day to the rest of the
country. And there is a drop in twinning
rate. But this similar drop is also seen
in the other part of the country, the
eastern part of the country where there
was no caloric deprivation. So, Ericson
concluded that nutrition seems to be of
minimal importance for producing twins.
Other environmental risk factors that
are quite well established are smoking
and uh body
composition, an increase in BMI, body
mass index and heavy smoking both are
risk factors quite well established risk
factors for dicycotic twinning. Although
we don't quite understand the mechanism
as those two risk factors are also
associated with uh
infertility. Very well documented. Uh
more twins with in increasing maternal
age and um as I mentioned before very
large global differences. Of course, I
label this environment, but we should

genomewide association studies in uh
human
genetics. Our colleague
uh at that time working at the FY
University in Amsterdam was brave enough
Hamdi Mubarak to initiate a G-W was

uh follicular stimulating hormone which

uh eggs in the mother. And a second

the response of the ovaries to FSH.
We did a study in which we tried to

and given the prevalence in the Dutch

present a woman has a 29% higher

And the work in human twinning including

again

because an important discussion in human

So

multiples that were born in 1934 in

It begins with a single
ovulation fertilization and
then somewhere between day three and day
12 after
fertilization there is a

anything what triggers the

It's very consistent uh globally and
yeah basically it's uh a big mystery.
There is a hypothesis that twins that

oneon are early
splitters. and when they have
uh different uh corionicity and two

But although this sounds very

So since there is so little evidence

a suggestion that epigenetics could be
involved. Um the incidence of

was quite
unexpected. So one example of an

t-fold um among patients there's an
almost t-fold increase of monocycotic

compared um genomewide metilation
patterns between mono and dicycodic

uh singleton

um we looked in two tissues and we

different
ages. So this is the paper that was

communications with Jenny Fondon as the

Um we started the project in the

before we obtained
243 genomewide significant hits. This is

834 differential metilated uh probes

So to summarize um we found a signature

The signature is almost strong enough,
